elements.sent-2411-001: Dynamic scene reconstruction is a long-term challenge in 3D vision.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent methods extend 3D Gaussian Splatting to dynamic scenes via additional deformation fields and apply explicit constraints like motion flow to guide the deformation.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, they learn motion changes from individual timestamps independently, making it challenging to reconstruct complex scenes, particularly when dealing with violent movement, extreme-shaped geometries, or reflective surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address the above issue, we design a plug-and-play module called \name to enable existing deformable 3D Gaussians reconstruction methods with the ability to implicitly model motion patterns from a learning perspective.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, \name includes a Cross-Temporal Transformer Encoder, which adaptively learns the temporal relationships of deformable 3D Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we propose a two-stream optimization strategy that transfers the motion knowledge learned from \name to the base stream during the training phase.
elements.sent-2411-007: This allows us to remove \name during inference, thereby preserving the original rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments in the multi-view and monocular dynamic scenes validate qualitative and quantitative improvement brought by \name.
elements.sent-2411-010: High-quality reconstruction of dynamic scenes is significantly challenging in computer vision and graphics, yet has a wide range of potential applications in movie production, virtual reality, and augmented reality.
elements.sent-2411-011: The difficulty stems from factors like occlusions, translucent materials, specular surfaces, and changing topology, all of which are prevalent in dynamic scenes.
elements.sent-2411-012: Inspired by the success of neural radiance field~(NeRF) on static scenes~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2021mip,muller2022instant , extending NeRF to dynamic scenes~\cite{li2022streaming,tian2023mononerf,zhou2024dynpoint,park2021hypernerf,park2021nerfies,liu2023robust,li2021neural,gao2021dynamic has been explored.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, these NeRF-based methods are limited by computationally intensive volume rendering~\cite{drebin1988volume , which makes real-time rendering almost impossible.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting~(3DGS)~\cite{kerbl3Dgaussians represents the scene with anisotropic 3D Gaussians and develops a rasterization-based rendering algorithm, which allows real-time rendering through directly projecting 3D Gaussians onto the image plane.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Following the revolutionized 3DGS, dynamic Gaussian Splatting methods~\cite{Li_STG_2024_CVPR,4drotorgs,yang2023gs4d,sun20243dgstream have been introduced for reconstructing dynamic scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-016: These methods typically construct a canonical 3DGS and utilize a deformation field to deform it based on individual timestamps~\cite{yang2023deformable3dgs, Li_STG_2024_CVPR, Wu_2024_CVPR .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, temporal relationships of 3D Gaussians have been poorly investigated.
elements.sent-2411-018: Previous studies have primarily modeled motion patterns using various types of the deformation field, for example, MLPs~\cite{luiten2024dynamic, bae2024per, lu20243d, shaw2024swings, yang2023deformable3dgs, huang2024sc,zhao2024gaussianprediction,wansuperpoint , spatial-temporal planes~\cite{Wu_2024_CVPR, lu2024dn, duisterhof2023md , polynomial functions~\cite{Li_STG_2024_CVPR , Fourier series~\cite{katsumata2024a , and combinations of these methods~\cite{lin2024gaussian .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: These methods learn motion patterns from independent time input in a vanilla way, neglecting internal cross-time relationships.
elements.sent-2411-020: Some studies introduce the motion flow regularization~\cite{Li2024ST to explicitly learn motion patterns from neighboring frames~\cite{luiten2024dynamic,lu2024dn .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Although these methods promote similar motion between adjacent timestamps, they adopt a local perspective on time series during optimization.
elements.sent-2411-022: This constraint makes it challenging to reconstruct scenes with more complex motion patterns, such as sudden appearances, violent movements, or reflective surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we introduce \name, a transformer module designed to implicitly learn motion patterns across multiple timestamps.
elements.sent-2411-024: By modeling temporal relationships within a time batch, we aim to provide a global view of the entire time series, enabling the deformation field backbones themselves to capture motion patterns from a learning perspective, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, \name utilizes a \encoder to capture the implicit motion patterns of 3D Gaussians across multiple sampled timestamps using a self-attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, to avoid additional computational costs of \name during inference, we present a two-stream optimization strategy.
elements.sent-2411-027: By sharing the weights of two deformation fields, we transfer the motion pattern learning from \name stream to the base stream during training.
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, we can eliminate \name during inference and maintain the original rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-029: Notably, \name does not require any prior information and extracts motion patterns solely from RGB supervision, which can be seamlessly adapted to previous deformable 3D Gaussian methods in a plug-and-play manner.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, \name promotes faster gradient descent and guides a more efficient canonical space, ultimately increasing FPS during inference, as demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: point_cloud .}
elements.sent-2411-031: To sum up, our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose \name, a transformer module that enhances current deformable 3D Gaussians reconstruction methods in a plug-and-play manner from an automatic learning perspective.
elements.sent-2411-033: The two-stream optimization strategy allows the exclusion of \name during inference while maintaining and even improving rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-034: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets validate the effectiveness of \name, achieving state-of-the-art rendering quality.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, trim=20 0 20 20, clip]{images/point_cloud.pdf} \caption{\name guides towards more efficiently distributed canonical space, showing higher FPS and better quality. The results are from ``cut lemon'' in the HyperNeRF Dataset~\cite{park2021hypernerf}.} \label{fig: point_cloud}
elements.sent-2411-035: Dynamic Scene Reconstruction has been extensively researched over many years, with a wealth of studies~\cite{kanade1997virtualized,zitnick2004high,park2021hypernerf,yang2023deformable3dgs,yang2002real contributing to current progress.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The pioneering neural radiance field~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf,barron2021mip,yu2021plenoctrees,sun2022direct,fridovich2022plenoxels has demonstrated photorealistic rendering for novel view synthesis from calibrated multiview images, inspiring extensive approaches~\cite{du2021neural,xian2021space,tretschk2021non, pumarola2021d,park2021nerfies, fang2022fast, park2021hypernerf to extend NeRF to 4D space-time field for dynamic scene reconstruction.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: There are majorly two lines of NeRF-based methods for dynamic scene reconstruction: 1) deformation-based methods~\cite{tretschk2021non, pumarola2021d,park2021nerfies, fang2022fast, park2021hypernerf model deformation changes by using a deformation field to map the queried positions in different timestamps to a canonical space; 2) compact 4D space-time fields~\cite{du2021neural,xian2021space,li2022nv3d,chen2022tensorf take the timestamps, positions, and directions as input to predict color and density.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, NeRF-based approaches are limited to training and rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, a large number of methods~\cite{li2022streaming, liu2022devrf, wang2022mixed, wang2023neural, shao2023tensor4d, fridovich2023k, cao2023hexplane, xu20244k4d, wu2024tetrirf, wang2024masked, song2023nerfplayer, attal2023hyperreel, lou2024darenerf have been proposed to accelerate NeRF-based methods.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Recent 3DGS~\cite{kerbl3Dgaussians achieves real-time rendering and dynamic 3DGS approaches~\cite{yang2023gs4d, yang2023deformable3dgs, Li_STG_2024_CVPR emerge.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In details, deformation-based methods~\cite{luiten2024dynamic, bae2024per, lu20243d, shaw2024swings, yang2023deformable3dgs, huang2024sc, zhao2024gaussianprediction, wansuperpoint, Wu_2024_CVPR, lu2024dn, duisterhof2023md, Li_STG_2024_CVPR, katsumata2024a, lin2024gaussian employ a deformation field to predict per-Gaussian offsets and deform a canonical 3DGS according to different timestamps.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Meanwhile, 4DGS~\cite{yang2023gs4d and 4D-Rotor~\cite{4drotorgs add the time dimension to 3D Gaussian, forming a 4D Gaussian representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: TimeFormer focuses on modeling the temporal relationship and is plug-and-play to deformation-based methods~\cite{yang2023deformable3dgs and 4D space-time representations~\cite{yang2023gs4d,Li_STG_2024_CVPR .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: There are also a few attempts~\cite{sun20243dgstream,shaw2024swings,lin2024gaussian in leveraging the nearby motion or flow for dynamic Gaussian reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream proposes a per-frame optimization strategy, which predicts current Gaussian attributes based on previous Gaussian attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Meanwhile, Gaussian-Flow~\cite{lin2024gaussian presents explicitly model time-dependent residual of each attribute.}
elements.sent-2411-047: To model a long video sequence, SWinGS~\cite{shaw2024swings splits a video sequence into multiple sliding windows based on optical flow and uses a deformation-based method to model each sliding window.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Motion prediction in dynamic scene reconstruction from multi-view videos or monocular videos is an ill-posed problem.
elements.sent-2411-049: Early dynamic NeRF~\cite{gao2021dynamic, tretschk2021non, park2021nerfies, park2021hypernerf directly learns motion patterns from individual timestamps , and several later works propose motion flow regularization terms~\cite{gao2021dynamic, li2021neural, song2022pref, tian2023mononerf, liu2023robust, park2023temporal, wang2023flow, somraj2024factorized, zhan2024kfd, miao2024ctnerf to promote the learning of cross-time motion patterns.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: These methods typically utilize 2D prior information (\eg, optical flow) from pre-trained networks~\cite{teed2020raft to supervise the scene flows within neighboring frames.}
elements.sent-2411-051: PREF~\cite{song2022pref uses motion predictor to infer current motion based on the previous four frames and propose a self-supervision strategy without optical flow prior.}
elements.sent-2411-052: KFD-NeRF~\cite{zhan2024kfd models motion patterns by Kalman Filter based on the previous two frames.}
elements.sent-2411-053: CT-NeRF~\cite{miao2024ctnerf employs a cross-attention mechanism from transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention to learn the correlation of conservative frames.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods~\cite{guo2024motion, wang2024gflow, gao2024Gaussianflow, zhu2024motiongs also utilize optical flow supervision to enhance reconstruction performance.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Techniques such as MD-Splatting~\cite{duisterhof2023md , D3DG~\cite{luiten2024dynamic , and ST-4DGS~\cite{Li2024ST focus on minimizing both forward and backward Gaussian flow to promote temporal smoothness in Gaussian motions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: DN-4DGS~\cite{lu2024dn incorporates information from previous, current, and next timestamps into the deformation field to capture cross-time motion patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, these methods primarily establish temporal correlations with neighboring timestamps , which limits their ability to address long-term motion changes.
elements.sent-2411-058: Additionally, they introduce extra computational costs during inference, which can reduce rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-059: In contrast, \name captures the temporal relationships of Gaussians from a global perspective across the entire time series.
elements.sent-2411-060: Notably, \name is employed only during the training phase, without any additional computational costs during inference.
elements.sent-2411-061: For a position $x \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times1}$ in 3D space, the corresponding contribution of a 3D Gaussian on it can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\label{equ: contri_gs} G(x) = o \cdot e^{ -\frac{1}{2} (x-\mu)^{\top} \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu) }.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ can be decomposed into a scaling matrix $\mathbf{S}$ and a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$: $\Sigma = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{S ^T\mathbf{R ^T$, where $\mathbf{S = \text{diag ([s_x, s_y, s_z])$ and $\mathbf{R}$ can be transformed from a quaternion $[r_w, r_x, r_y, r_z]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Then the 3D Gaussians can be splatted to a 2D camera plane through differential Gaussian splatting.
elements.sent-2411-064: To model the appearance of 3D Gaussians, spherical harmonics (SH) are introduced to define the color $c$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Finally, for each pixel, the rendering results of 3DGS can be derived by calculating the color contribution of all the related Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-066: This process is known as $\alpha$-blending: \begin{equation}\label{formula: splatting volume rendering} C = \sum_{i}^N c_i \alpha_i \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} (1-\alpha_j),\end{equation} where $c_i$, $\alpha_i$ represent the color and density computed from the $i$-th 3D Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-067: Recent methods~\cite{yang2023deformable3dgs, Wu_2024_CVPR, Li_STG_2024_CVPR utilize deformation fields that extend 3DGS to 4D space, inspired from NeRF-based methods such as D-NeRF~\cite{pumarola2021d .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The structure of the deformation field $\mathcal{D}$ can vary among MLP~\cite{yang2023deformable3dgs , K-Plane~\cite{Wu_2024_CVPR and Polynominal~\cite{Li_STG_2024_CVPR , while the deformation process can be summarized as follows: \begin{equation}(\Delta \mu, \Delta r, \Delta s) = \mathcal{D}(\mu, t) , \label{eq: deform}\end{equation} where timestamp $t \in \mathcal{T}$, $\mathcal{T \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times 1}$ contains $T$ linear time inputs, $\mu, r, s$ are the center position, rotation quaternion, and scaling factors of 3D Gaussians, and $\Delta \mu, \Delta r, \Delta s$ are their residuals, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The inputs and outputs of Eq.~\ref{eq: deform can vary among different methods, while they share the same framework.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/framework.pdf} \caption{The Framework of Deformable 3D Gaussians Reconstruction with \name. Existing deformable 3D Gaussians framework usually includes the canonical space and the deformation field (first row), we incorporate \name to capture cross-time relationships and explore motion patterns implicitly (second row). We share weights of two deformation fields to transfer the learned motion knowledge. This allows us to exclude this Auxiliary Training Module during inference. } \label{fig: framework}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{images/gs_timeformer.pdf} \caption{The Structure of \encoder. We concatenate randomly sampled timestamps to position $\mu$, treating them as special tokens in a sequence. This module is designed to model multi-temporal relationships and produce distinct time-variant position offsets $\Delta p_0, \dots, \Delta p_{B-1}$. } \label{fig: gs_timeformer}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{images/flow_change.pdf} \caption{Data Flow Changes in the Deformation Field. Dashed lines represent new data flow among time samples $t_0, \dots,t_{B-1}$.} \label{fig: flow}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we first provide an overview of the enhanced deformable 3D Gaussians reconstruction with the proposed \name (Sec.~\ref{sec: overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-071: We then introduce the \name in detail (Sec.~\ref{sec: gs_timeformer ) which consists of a \encoder and shared deformation fields (Sec.~\ref{sec: shared ).}}
elements.sent-2411-072: We also discuss the insights behind \name from the view of gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-073: Previous methods model motion patterns by explicitly learning temporal relationships on individual or neighboring timestamps, failing on those complex scenes containing violent movement or dynamic reflective surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-074: In contrast, we present \name to enable the deformable 3D Gaussian backbones themselves to model cross-time relationships from an implicit learning perspective.
elements.sent-2411-075: The main framework with the proposed \name is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: framework .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Our approach retains standard reconstruction modules, which include (1) 3D Gaussians in the canonical space and (2) a deformation field that applies time-variant transformation.
elements.sent-2411-077: Additionally, \name is introduced before the deformation field to extract implicit cross-time motion features for each Gaussian through a self-attention mechanism along the time dimension.
elements.sent-2411-078:
Moreover, we share the weights of two deformation fields to transfer the motion knowledge from 
ame to mitigate the gap between the original branch and 
ame branch, which supports real-time rendering without 
ame during inference.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let $\mathcal{T _s \subset \mathcal{T , \mathcal{T _s = \{t_i\}_{i=0 ^{B-1}$ denotes randomly sampled timestamps, and let $\mathcal{G \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times (3+C)}$ denotes Gaussians in the canonical space, where $B$ is the size of time batch, $N$ is the number of Gaussians, $3+C$ means each Gaussian has 3 position channels and $C$ additional channels.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: As in Fig.~\ref{fig: gs_timeformer , all Gaussians's postions are made into $B$ copies, expanded into $\mathcal{G _c \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times N \times 3}$, and sampled timestamps are made into $N$ copies, expanded into $\mathcal{T' \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times N \times 1}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082:
We treat $[\gamma(\mathcal{G _c, \gamma(\mathcal{T' )] \in \mathbb{R ^{B 	imes N 	imes (3	imes 2
umfrequencies+ 2
umfrequencies) }$ as the original input $F_0$ to 
ame.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: It can be considered as $N$ sequences of the length $B$, containing $8\numfrequencies$ feature channels.
elements.sent-2411-084: With $M$ transformer encoder layers, for $m^{th}$ layer, intermediate features are calculated through multi-head self-attention(MSA) and MLP blocks.
elements.sent-2411-085: In the final stage, we use a tiny MLP to transform the last encoded features $F_{M-1}$ into offset $\mathcal{O \in \mathbb{R ^{B \times N \times 3}$ in the linear space: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{O}=MLP(F_{M-1}), \quad \mathcal{G}_t = \mathcal{G}_c + \mathcal{O} \end{aligned} \label{eq: offset}\end{equation} We consider the output from the \encoder as a fixing residual term to the original positions to encourage a gradual, steady learning process on motion patterns.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Implicit cross-time relationship learning in \name enables the automatic aggregation of Gaussians with similar variations during optimization, promoting more efficient spatial distribution and accelerating rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-087: Gradient Analysis Let $\mathcal{D}$ and $\mathcal{P}$ be the deformation field and \name respectively, the output of the deformation field $\Delta \mu_i$ and partial derivative for \( \mu \) in time $t_i \in \mathcal{T _s$ are formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta \mu_i = \mathcal{D}(\mu, t_i), \quad \frac{\partial \Delta \mu_i}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{D}(\mu, t_i)}{\partial \mu} \label{eq: backward_ori}\end{equation} With \name applied on time batch $\mathcal{T _s$, we reformulate as Eq.~\ref{eq: forward_ours and data flow changes as in Fig.~\ref{fig: flow . \begin{equation}\Delta \mu_i = \mathcal{D}(\mu + \mathcal{P}(\mu, \mathcal{T}_s), t_i) \label{eq: forward_ours}\end{equation} To calculate the partial derivative for $\mu$, we apply the chain rule.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Let $a = \mu + \mathcal{P (\mu, \mathcal{T _s)$, then: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \Delta \mu_i}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{D}(a, t_i)}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial \mu}, \quad \frac{\partial a}{\partial \mu} = 1 + \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}(\mu, \mathcal{T}_s)}{\partial \mu}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \Delta \mu_i}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{D}(a, t_i)}{\partial a} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}(\mu, \mathcal{T}_s)}{\partial \mu}\right) \label{eq: backward_ours}\end{equation} Eq.~\ref{eq: backward_ori has a weaker dynamic nature, as it only considers the current timestamp $t_i$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \Delta \mu_i}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial D(a, t_i)}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial \mu}, \quad \frac{\partial a}{\partial \mu} = 1 + \frac{\partial P(\mu, T_s)}{\partial \mu}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \Delta \mu_i}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial D(a, t_i)}{\partial a} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial P(\mu, T_s)}{\partial \mu}\right) \label{eq: backward_ours}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: In contrast, Eq.~\ref{eq: backward_ours incorporates an additional gradient term, $\frac{\partial \mathcal{P (\mu, \mathcal{T _s) {\partial \mu}$, enabling the current state to be influenced by any past or future states.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In other words, $\Delta \mu_i$ also optimizes the models according to other timestamps $t_j \in \mathcal{T _s (j \neq i)$, which is a significant difference compared to the backward process without \name.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Such design accounts for cross-time attention and allows the models to capture more challenging motion patterns from a global view of the entire time series.
elements.sent-2411-092: Accounting for additional computation costs of \name, which can significantly decrease rendering speed, we force the original deformation field and the auxiliary deformation field to share weights for knowledge transferring.
elements.sent-2411-093: Let $\mathcal{V}$ be the camera viewpoints, we apply the shared deformation fields to predict deformed space from both Gaussians in the canonical space and Gaussians from \name.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then, we apply the splatting algorithm to these two groups of deformed space.
elements.sent-2411-095: We calculate the losses between rendered images and ground truth $\mathcal{I _{gt}$ as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_c = \Vert Splatting(\mathcal{D}(\mathcal{G}_c, \mathcal{T}) , \mathcal{V}) -\mathcal{I}_{gt} \Vert_1\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_t = \Vert Splatting(\mathcal{D}(\mathcal{G}_t, \mathcal{T}) , \mathcal{V}) -\mathcal{I}_{gt} \Vert_1\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \lambda_c \mathcal{L}_c + \lambda_t \mathcal{L}_t\end{equation} , where $\mathcal{L _c, \mathcal{L _t$ represent losses of original branch and \name branch with $\lambda_c > \lambda_t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We use a relatively smaller $\lambda_t$ because we find it easy to overfit on the second branch with \name, causing a degradation in inference quality.

elements.sent-2411-142: The Transformer Encoder receives input structured as [seq\_len, seq\_batch, channel], and we input x structured as [$B$, $N$, 4], where $B$ is the size of time batch, $N$ is the number of Gaussians and 4 means 3 position channel and 1 channel for the timestamp.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \algsetup{linenosize= } \SetAlFnt{ } \SetAlCapFnt{ } \SetAlCapNameFnt{ } \SetAlgoLined \PyCode{class \name(nn.module):} \Indp \PyComment{d\_in: here is 4, (x, y, z, t)} \PyComment{L: frequency of Position Encoding (PE) $\gamma$} \PyCode{def \_\_init\_\_(self, d\_in, L, nhead, d\_hidden, n\_layer):} \Indp \PyComment{PE: PE function, PE\_ch: d\_in$\times$2L} \PyCode{self.PE, PE\_ch = get\_PE(L=L, d\_in=d\_in)} \PyComment{define \encoder} \PyCode{layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(PE\_ch, nhead, d\_hidden, activation=nn.functional.tanh)} \PyCode{self.encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(layer, n\_layer)} \PyComment{define Tiny MLP} \PyCode{self.mlp = nn.Linear(PE\_ch, 3)} \Indm \PyCode{} \PyComment{x: [seq\_len, seq\_batch, channel] } \PyCode{def forward(self, x):} \Indp \PyCode{PE\_x = self.PE(x)} \PyCode{h = self.encoder(PE\_x)} \PyCode{h = self.mlp(h)} \PyCode{return h} \Indm \Indm \caption{Implementation of \name.} \label{alg: timeformer_code}

elements.sent-2411-156: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-158: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-159: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-160: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-161: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-162: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-163: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-164: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-165: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-166: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-167: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-168: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-169: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-170: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-171: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-172: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-174: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-177: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-178: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-179: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-180: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-181: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-182: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-183: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-019: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-185: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-188: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-189: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-190: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-191: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-192: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
